summary:
The electrical conductivity speed is fast due to being a large diameter myelinated fibre involved in fine touch sensations; estimated around mid-range within normal myelinated nerves but not reaching top end as seen in some other types such as those responsible for pain or movement control signals at about 70m/s typically while still significantly higher than unmyelinated ones which conduct much slower by contrast.
To refine this summary into two concise sentences focusing only on critical information:

A-alpha fibers have very rapid conduction speeds because they are large myelinated neurons primarily associated with precise tactile senses. Their exact speed likely ranges between approximately 30–60 meters per second based on comparisons with known myelinated nerve conductions. To ensure clarity and relevance directly addressing your query without extraneous details: 

Key Points: 
- Rapid conduction
- Large myelinated neuron type  
- Speed range estimate provided

This distills Agent1’s detailed explanation down to just core facts pertinent to understanding why these specific nerve fibers exhibit quick signal transmission capabilities relative to size/diameter/classification characteristics compared against general categories of neural tissue behavior regarding impulse propagation rates across different anatomical contexts.
